Every review comment made me a slightly better engineerWhen I started contributing to Checkstyle, my goal was simple:
Get my pull request merged.
Like most beginners, I thought success in open source meant writing code that worked. If the tests passed and the implementation solved the issue, I assumed the pull request would be accepted.
I quickly learned that open source doesn’t work that way.
The first few review comments I received weren’t about bugs.
They were about writing better code.
And that was the beginning of one of the biggest shifts in my engineering journey.
“It Works” Isn’t the Finish Line
One of the biggest differences between college projects and mature open-source projects is the definition of “done.”
In college, if the output was correct, the assignment was complete.
In a mature project like Checkstyle, correctness is only one part of the equation.
Maintainers also care about questions like:

• Is this solution maintainable?

• Does it follow the project’s architecture?

• What happens in edge cases?

• Can another contributor understand this six months later?

• Does this change accidentally affect existing behavior?

• Have you added the right tests?
Those weren’t questions I naturally asked myself when I started. Today, they are some of the first questions I think about before writing code.
Every Review Was a Mini Design Discussion
Initially, I saw review comments as corrections.
Now, I see them as engineering discussions.
Sometimes a maintainer would suggest a completely different implementation.
Sometimes they would recommend simplifying logic that I thought was already simple.
Other times, they would ask for additional test cases covering scenarios I hadn’t even considered.
At first, I wondered:
“Why not just merge it if it works?”
Later, I realized something important.
The maintainers weren’t reviewing just my code. They were protecting the quality of a project that thousands of developers rely on.
Once I started viewing reviews from that perspective, everything changed.
The Best Feedback Wasn’t Always About Code
Some of the most valuable feedback I received had nothing to do with Java syntax.
It was about communication.
I learned to explain:

• why I chose a particular approach,

• what alternatives I considered,

• why certain edge cases were intentionally handled,

• and how my implementation aligned with the project’s existing patterns.
Writing a pull request description became more than just explaining what I changed.
It became an opportunity to communicate my thought process.
That habit has helped me just as much in my professional work as it has in open source.
I Started Reading Other People’s Reviews
One habit that accelerated my learning more than I expected was reading review discussions on pull requests that weren’t mine.
Initially, I only followed conversations on my own contributions. Eventually, I started reading reviews across the repository.
I noticed recurring patterns.
The same suggestions appeared again and again:

• simplify the implementation,

• avoid unnecessary complexity,

• write clearer tests,

• preserve existing behavior,

• make changes easier to maintain.
It felt like attending hundreds of small engineering lessons completely free.
Some of the best things I learned about software engineering came from code reviews on pull requests I never wrote.
Open Source Changed How I Review My Own Code
Over time, something interesting happened.
Before opening a pull request, I found myself asking the same questions I expected maintainers to ask.

• Is there a simpler way?

• Have I missed an edge case?

• Will this break another feature?

• Are the tests meaningful?

• Can someone unfamiliar with this code understand it?
Without realizing it, I had started reviewing my own work before anyone else did. That single habit reduced review iterations and improved the quality of my contributions significantly.
The Impact Beyond Open Source
One unexpected outcome of this journey was how much it influenced my day-to-day work as a software engineer.
Whether I’m working on backend services, automation, or internal tools, I now spend much more time thinking about maintainability than simply making something work.
I communicate implementation decisions more clearly.
I write tests more deliberately.
And I approach reviews as collaborative discussions rather than approval checkpoints.
Those habits didn’t come from a course or a textbook.
They came from months of interacting with experienced maintainers who cared deeply about code quality.
Advice for New Contributors
If you’re new to open source, don’t fear review comments. In fact, hope you get them.
A pull request that receives thoughtful feedback is often more valuable than one that’s merged immediately. Every comment is an opportunity to learn how experienced engineers think.
And that’s something no tutorial can teach.
Looking back, getting my pull requests merged felt rewarding.
But the real value wasn’t the green “Merged” button.
It was everything I learned before reaching it.

Next in the series
By this point, I had become comfortable contributing to open source but there was one tool I still relied on every single day: the terminal.
When I started, the only Git command I knew was git status.
Months later, I found myself using Git almost instinctively, not because I had memorized commands, but because open source made them part of my daily workflow.
In the next article, I’ll share the Git commands that open source taught me naturally, and why I no longer think of Git as something to memorize.
